title: Perspectives on globalisation
summary: 9699 — definitions, causes, and competing theories of globalisation.
learningObjectives: Define globalisation and its key dimensions.
Explain economic, cultural, and political globalisation.
Compare hyperglobalist, sceptic, and transformationalist views.
Evaluate whether globalisation is a new or continuing process.

simpleExplanation.title: A smaller, connected world
simpleExplanation.summary: Globalisation links societies through trade, media, migration, and politics — but theorists disagree how deep and irreversible the change is.
steps: Economic — TNCs, free trade, global markets.

flashcards.back: Borders matter less — integrated global economy and culture (Ohmae).
Held — regionalisation; nation-states and trade blocs still dominant.
Globalisation reshapes but does not erase national societies.
Spread of ideas, media, consumer culture across borders.
Global products adapted to local cultures (Robertson).
Harvey — technology shrinks distance, speeds communication.

sections.content: **Globalisation perspectives** (7.1) opens Paper 4. Define terms before comparing **hyperglobalist vs sceptic vs transformationalist**.

sections.question: Assess the view that globalisation is creating a single global culture. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Homogenisation case:** **Americanisation** — McDonaldization (Ritzer); global brands; Western media dominance; loss of local languages/traditions.

**Counter-arguments:** **Glocalisation** — local adaptation; **hybridity** (Hall); **resistance** to cultural imperialism; **digital divide** limits access.

**Evaluation:** **Shared consumer culture** in some urban elites globally, but **deep local diversity** persists — **hybrid** not uniform culture.

**Conclusion:** **Partial cultural convergence**, not single global culture.
